name: Great Rhododendron
summary: Native to cool moist acidic ravines and north-facing slopes — the Pocono Plateau is core PA range. Large evergreen broad-leaved shrub or small tree. Late June–July pink-white flower trusses are visited by bumblebees, ruby-throated hummingbirds, and swallowtails.
notes: Dense evergreen winter structure for grouse and songbirds. Can form thicket-like stands in ravines that suppress tree regeneration if overdone — plant SPARINGLY (3–5 in a single ravine, not a dozen). The hemlock-rhododendron community is a Pocono signature where it still survives HWA-killed hemlocks. Pair sparingly to fill the cool-ravine niche.

planting.aspect: Cool ravines; north-facing slopes; near-stream seeps
planting.site_notes: Plant SPARINGLY (3–5 plants per cool ravine site) to avoid creating regeneration-suppressing thickets — same caution applies as mountain laurel. Wire cage; some deer browse on new growth. Source from native nurseries — most commercial rhododendrons are hybrids, not the wild species.
